book.title: The Aesop for Children
book.source: Aesop, ill. Milo Winter (Rand McNally, 1919). Public domain in the USA. Text via Project Gutenberg #19994.

level.title: The Wolf And The Lean Dog
level.moral: Take what you can get when you can get it.

segments.value: and bony Dog, and Master Wolf would have
meager fare had he not been
to edge toward the Dog, while the Dog
your lordship," said the Dog, his
interrupted now and then as he

a snap of the Wolf's teeth, "how unpleasant it would be to eat me now. Look at my ribs. I am nothing but skin and bone. But let me tell you something in private. In a few days my master will give a wedding feast for his only daughter. You can guess how fine and fat I will grow on the scraps from the table. Then is the time to eat me."

The Wolf could not help thinking how nice it would be to have a fine fat Dog to eat instead of the scrawny object before him. So he went away pulling in his belt and promising to return.

Some days later the Wolf came back for the promised feast. He found the Dog in his master's yard, and asked him to come out and be eaten.

"Sir," said the Dog, with a grin, "I shall be delighted to have you eat me. I'll be out as soon as the porter opens the door."

But the "porter" was a huge Dog whom the Wolf knew by painful experience to be very unkind toward wolves. So he decided not to wait and made off as fast as his legs could carry him.

Do not depend on the promises of those whose interest it is to deceive you.
